analysis.primary_boundary: Cathode decomposition threshold is near 250°C; below this, anode-electrolyte interface reactions dominate. Activating cathode reactions earlier violates the evidence-defined regime boundary.
intended_error_modes: Ignoring temperature-regime boundaries and treating all reactions as simultaneously active
Confusing positive-electrolyte reactions (130–170°C) with cathode active material decomposition (~250°C)
Accepting the proposal because cathode reactions eventually contribute, without checking the threshold condition

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery pack is subjected to thermal abuse inside a confined aircraft cabin. Batteries are positioned at a corner adjacent to a wall. Natural ventilation is the only active airflow mechanism. As the number of simultaneously venting cells increases from a single cell to several dozen cells, an engineer must assess which conclusions about gas hazard are admissible. Which of the following statements are supported by engineering reasoning under these conditions?
options: A. The hazardous flammable gas volume scales nonlinearly with cell count, and the duration of hazardous conditions also increases, compounding risk beyond what single-cell extrapolation would predict.
B. Corner and wall-adjacent placement suppresses gas accumulation by directing venting gases away from occupied zones, reducing peak concentration relative to centrally located packs.

C. Buoyancy-driven stratification causes high-concentration gas to persist near the ceiling rather than dispersing uniformly, creating a localized hazard zone that surface-level sensors may not detect.

D. Under natural ventilation, increasing the number of venting cells from one to several dozen causes only a proportional, linear increase in hazardous volume, so single-cell test data is sufficient for multi-cell risk assessment.
E. The corner placement of the pack tends to promote localized gas accumulation rather than suppress it, which is mechanistically consistent with reduced mixing from boundary walls.

F. Because buoyancy effects are dominant, forced ventilation at any flow rate will fully eliminate ceiling-level stratification and reduce peak concentration proportionally with increasing flow speed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows hazardous volume grows from ~0.09 m³ (1 cell) to >8 m³ (36 cells) with prolonged duration, confirming nonlinear scaling.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states corner/wall placement induces localized accumulation, not suppression; this option reverses the direction of the effect.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Buoyancy causes persistent ceiling stratification; surface-level sensors would underestimate ceiling concentration, a valid diagnostic limitation inference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly shows disproportionate scaling (0.09 to >8 m³), contradicting linear extrapolation from single-cell data.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states corner/wall packs induce localized accumulation; reduced mixing from boundaries is the consistent mechanistic explanation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows increasing ventilation rate beyond a threshold yields no further reduction in peak concentration or duration, so proportional improvement is unsupported.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of multi-cell gas hazard scaling and spatial accumulation conclusions under natural ventilation in confined spaces
analysis.primary_boundary: Nonlinear scaling vs. linear extrapolation; corner placement promotes rather than suppresses accumulation; ventilation has a saturation threshold
intended_error_modes: mechanism_reversal on corner placement
unjustified linear extrapolation from single-cell data
protective mechanism overclaim for forced ventilation
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming forced ventilation eliminates stratification at any flow rate
Treating single-cell hazard volume as proportionally scalable to multi-cell scenarios

solver_view.stem: An engineer evaluates two ventilation layout configurations for a confined cabin environment following battery thermal runaway gas release. Layout I (conventional) requires 2.5 m/s to achieve acceptable hazardous zone control. Layout II (optimized) achieves comparable hazardous zone control at 1.5 m/s. When Layout II is further increased to 2.5 m/s, peak concentration is similar to that at 1.5 m/s and duration does not decrease. The engineer must decide which design and operational conclusions are admissible. Select all admissible conclusions.
options: A. Layout II at 1.5 m/s is preferable to Layout I at 2.5 m/s on an energy-efficiency basis, since both achieve comparable hazardous zone control.

B. The diminishing returns observed when Layout II is increased from 1.5 to 2.5 m/s indicate that ventilation layout geometry, not flow speed alone, is the primary determinant of hazardous zone control beyond a certain flow threshold.
C. Because Layout II at 2.5 m/s fails to outperform Layout II at 1.5 m/s, increasing flow speed is universally ineffective for hazardous gas mitigation regardless of layout configuration.
D. Layout I at 2.5 m/s and Layout II at 1.5 m/s produce equivalent safety outcomes, so either can be selected without penalty when energy consumption is not a constraint.
E. The saturation behavior of Layout II above 1.5 m/s implies that a minimum effective ventilation speed exists for this layout, below which hazardous zone control would likely degrade.
F. The evidence supports recommending Layout II at 2.5 m/s as the optimal configuration because it combines the best layout geometry with the highest tested flow speed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states Layout II at 1.5 m/s achieves similar control as Layout I at 2.5 m/s; energy efficiency advantage is a minimal valid implication.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Saturation at 1.5 m/s for Layout II while Layout I requires 2.5 m/s supports layout geometry as the dominant factor beyond a flow threshold.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Saturation is observed only for Layout II above 1.5 m/s; universalizing to all layouts and flow speeds is an unsupported overgeneralization.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence notes Layout II at 1.5 m/s achieves ~0.27% additional peak concentration reduction; equivalence is approximate, not exact, making this a conservative but imprecise overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Saturation behavior above 1.5 m/s logically implies a threshold; below it, control would degrade. This is a boundary reasoning implication consistent with the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows Layout II at 2.5 m/s provides no improvement over 1.5 m/s; recommending it as optimal ignores the saturation finding and wastes energy.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of ventilation design conclusions given saturation behavior and layout-dependent performance
analysis.primary_boundary: Saturation of Layout II above 1.5 m/s applies only to that layout; cannot universalize; equivalence of layouts is approximate not exact
intended_error_modes: overgeneralization of saturation to all layouts
exact equivalence overclaim
recommending higher flow as optimal despite saturation evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Universalizing Layout II saturation behavior to Layout I or other configurations
Claiming Layout II at 2.5 m/s is optimal

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery is subjected to thermal runaway under hydraulic oil immersion. At 25 mm and 45 mm immersion heights, no thermal runaway occurs (peak temperature rise rates below 1°C/s), but the fractional effective dose (FED) for gas asphyxiation remains above 1. At 75 mm full immersion, FED drops below 1 and hydrocarbon gas generation is eliminated. Separately, as SOC increases from 0% to 100% under immersion, peak surface temperature decreases while HF concentration increases. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Preventing thermal runaway through partial immersion is not sufficient to eliminate serious asphyxiation hazard, because FED can remain above 1 even when temperature rise rates are well below the thermal runaway threshold.
B. At higher SOC under immersion, the reduction in peak surface temperature indicates reduced electrochemical energy release, which also reduces HF generation as a correlated byproduct.

C. Full immersion eliminates the flammability hazard from hydrocarbon gases and reduces asphyxiation risk below the critical FED threshold, but these benefits are achieved simultaneously only at the 75 mm depth.

D. The increase in HF concentration with increasing SOC under immersion conditions suggests that the mechanism driving HF generation is distinct from the mechanism driving peak temperature, since the two trends move in opposite directions.
E. Because partial immersion suppresses thermal runaway, it also guarantees that all toxic gas hazards are reduced to safe levels, making full immersion unnecessary from a safety standpoint.

F. The non-monotonic relationship between SOC and hazard profile under immersion means that optimizing immersion depth for temperature suppression alone may not minimize the overall chemical hazard to personnel.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows FED >1 at partial immersion despite no thermal runaway; thermal runaway suppression and asphyxiation hazard are decoupled.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows HF increases with SOC while temperature decreases; attributing HF reduction to temperature reduction directly contradicts the observed trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows hydrocarbon elimination and FED<1 both occur at 75 mm; stating both benefits require 75 mm is supported and condition-specific.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Opposite SOC trends for surface temperature and HF concentration support mechanistic decoupling; this is a valid multi-factor interaction inference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly shows FED remains above 1 under partial immersion despite no thermal runaway; this option is conservative-sounding but factually contradicted.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Temperature and HF move in opposite directions with SOC; optimizing depth for temperature alone ignores the HF hazard increase, a valid multi-factor trade-off conclusion.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of safety conclusions when thermal runaway suppression and toxic gas hazards are mechanistically decoupled under immersion conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: Thermal runaway suppression ≠ elimination of all gas hazards; HF and temperature trends are opposite with SOC
intended_error_modes: conflating thermal runaway suppression with full hazard elimination
assuming HF correlates positively with temperature
overclaiming partial immersion sufficiency
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring that any immersion depth eliminating thermal runaway also eliminates toxic gas hazard
Treating temperature reduction as a proxy for all hazard reduction under SOC variation

solver_view.prompt: Ejected powder from lithium-ion battery thermal runaway contains carbon as its dominant component by mass. In an inert (argon) atmosphere, the first-step weight loss of this powder during heating decreases as SOC increases from 40% to 100%. In an air atmosphere, first-step weight loss increases with increasing heating power at fixed SOC and temperature. However, dust explosion testing across a concentration range of 30–2000 g/m³ produced no explosion pressure exceeding 0.05 MPa. Explain the mechanistic reasoning that connects particle size to the absence of dust explosion, and identify one condition under which the explosion hazard conclusion might no longer be admissible. Limit your answer to the key physical mechanism and the specific boundary condition.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The ejected powder particles are predominantly in the 147–210 µm size range without grinding or processing. Particles of this size cannot form a stable suspension in air, which is a prerequisite for dust explosion propagation. Without stable suspension, the powder-air mixture cannot maintain the uniform dispersion needed to sustain a deflagration front, so no explosion pressure above 0.05 MPa was observed across the tested concentration range. The admissibility of this conclusion is bounded by the particle size condition: if the powder were subjected to grinding, pulverization, or sieving that reduced particle size substantially, stable suspension could become possible, and the no-explosion conclusion would no longer be admissible.
must_include: particle size in the 147–210 µm range prevents stable suspension in air
stable suspension is required for dust explosion propagation
the no-explosion conclusion is conditional on the powder remaining unprocessed
grinding or pulverization would invalidate the conclusion by enabling stable suspension
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming the powder is non-flammable in all forms or conditions
Attributing the absence of explosion to chemical inertness of the powder components
Generalizing the no-explosion conclusion to processed or size-reduced powder
Inferring that concentration has no effect on hazard independent of particle size

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic link between particle size, suspension stability, and dust explosion admissibility boundary
analysis.primary_boundary: No-explosion conclusion is valid only for unprocessed large-particle powder; grinding invalidates it by enabling stable suspension
intended_error_modes: Attributing explosion absence to chemical composition rather than physical suspension behavior
Treating the no-explosion result as unconditional across all particle sizes
Ignoring the processing-state boundary condition

solver_view.stem: A battery module operates under 3C discharge rate with forced convective cooling at 0.5 m/s flow speed using air at 1 bar ambient pressure. Thermal runaway propagation (TRP) is observed between adjacent cells. An engineer proposes two independent interventions: (I) switch the cooling medium from air to nitrogen at identical flow speed and pressure, and (II) increase ambient pressure to 3 bar while retaining air at 0.5 m/s. Which conclusions about these interventions are admissible?
options: A. Intervention I reduces TRP risk primarily because nitrogen has higher heat capacity than air, not because of a difference in flow velocity.
B. Intervention II suppresses TRP completely, but this outcome is attributable to increased fluid density enhancing heat transfer capability rather than a chemical suppression mechanism.
C. Intervention I is more effective than Intervention II because gas chemistry governs TRP suppression more fundamentally than pressure-driven density changes.
D. Intervention II would be ineffective unless flow speed is simultaneously increased beyond 0.5 m/s, since pressure alone cannot alter heat transfer at fixed velocity.
E. Both interventions suppress TRP through identical physical mechanisms, differing only in the magnitude of heat dissipation enhancement.
F. Intervention I cannot completely suppress TRP under 3C discharge rate regardless of nitrogen concentration, since only pressure elevation to 3 bar achieves complete inhibition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states nitrogen's superiority over air is due to its higher heat capacity, not flow velocity differences.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states 3 bar completely inhibits TRP and attributes this to increased fluid density raising heat transfer capability.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports both interventions work but does not establish a hierarchy between gas chemistry and pressure as more fundamental mechanisms.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows pressure elevation to 3 bar at fixed 0.5 m/s completely inhibits TRP; no co-requirement for increased flow speed is stated.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Nitrogen acts via heat capacity; pressure acts via fluid density increase. Mechanisms differ; conflating them is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows nitrogen reduces TRP risk but does not state it cannot completely suppress TRP under 3C; this is an unsupported absolute claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing physical mechanisms of two independent TRP suppression interventions and evaluating admissibility of mechanistic attribution claims
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism attribution must match the specific physical pathway stated in evidence; cross-intervention comparisons of effectiveness require explicit evidential support
intended_error_modes: mechanism_substitution
unsupported_hierarchical_comparison
condition_invention_for_pressure_effect
disallowed_extrapolations: Ranking nitrogen vs. pressure as more fundamental without evidence
Requiring flow speed increase as co-condition for pressure effect

solver_view.stem: In a confined battery enclosure, three cell configurations are tested: closely nested (no gap), 2 mm air gap, and 2 mm FR-PP thermal barrier. The FR-PP material melts between 148–160°C, begins decomposing at 280°C, and forms an insulating char up to 500°C. The air gap configuration shows radiation-dominated heat transfer with convective contributions only during venting. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. The FR-PP barrier prevents propagation partly because its phase-change absorption and subsequent char formation provide two sequential thermal resistance mechanisms rather than a single one.
B. In the air gap configuration, eliminating the venting/breaching phase would not significantly alter the dominant heat transfer mode, since radiation governs throughout the process.

C. The closely nested configuration produces higher peak enclosure pressure and temperature than the air gap configuration, indicating that direct cell contact accelerates both thermal and mechanical energy release.
D. The FR-PP barrier's effectiveness is primarily attributable to its convective blocking capability during venting, since radiation is already the dominant mode in the air gap scenario.
E. Because radiation dominates in the air gap scenario, replacing the air gap with a radiation-absorbing solid material would necessarily outperform the FR-PP barrier in all propagation metrics.
F. The 2 mm air gap delays propagation relative to closely nested cells because it eliminates conductive heat transfer between cells in direct contact.
G. The FR-PP barrier can be considered effective only if enclosure peak temperatures remain below its decomposition onset at 280°C, since char formation is not a designed protective function.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence describes phase-change latent heat absorption followed by decomposition with char formation, constituting two sequential thermal resistance stages.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states radiation dominates throughout TRP; convection from venting is additional but not the dominant mode, so its removal would not change dominance.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states closely nested cells show highest peak enclosure pressure and temperature compared to other configurations.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: FR-PP effectiveness is not attributed to convective blocking; evidence shows radiation dominates, so a convective-blocking mechanism attribution is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence does not support that any radiation-absorbing material outperforms FR-PP; this is an unsupported system-level generalization.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes the longer delay in the 2 mm air gap to the absence of conductive heat transfer from direct cell-to-cell contact.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence describes char formation as a functional protective property of FR-PP; framing it as non-designed contradicts the stated material behavior.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-mechanism evaluation of thermal barrier and air gap effectiveness, distinguishing dominant vs. secondary heat transfer modes and sequential protective stages
analysis.primary_boundary: Mechanism attribution must align with stated dominant heat transfer mode; FR-PP protection involves multiple sequential stages not reducible to one mechanism
intended_error_modes: mechanism_misattribution_to_convection
unsupported_generalization_from_radiation_dominance
conservative_scope_restriction_on_char_function
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming any radiation-absorbing material outperforms FR-PP
Restricting FR-PP effectiveness to sub-decomposition temperatures

solver_view.stem: During thermal runaway in a lithium-ion cell, the maximum heat generation rate reaches approximately 2862 W. A water mist cooling system delivers a peak cooling power of about 115.7 W during the TR period. A BN nanofluid spray system achieves a maximum heat dissipation power of 6067 W and cools the cell from peak TR temperature (~525°C) to 100°C in 11 s, compared to 20 s for water mist. An engineer claims the nanofluid system can suppress ongoing thermal runaway. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Neither water mist nor nanofluid spray can suppress thermal runaway during the active TR period, because even the nanofluid's peak cooling power is substantially below the heat generation rate during TR.

B. The nanofluid system's faster post-TR cooling compared to water mist is consistent with its higher maximum cooling rate, but this advantage applies to the cooling phase after TR, not during active TR suppression.
C. Because nanofluid spray dissipates 22 times more power than natural cooling, it can suppress thermal runaway onset if applied before SEI decomposition initiates the self-heating sequence.

D. The engineer's claim that nanofluid suppresses ongoing TR is admissible because the nanofluid's maximum heat dissipation power of 6067 W exceeds the water mist cooling power by a factor sufficient to cross the suppression threshold.
E. The nanofluid system's ability to suppress TR at a higher critical temperature than water mist implies it can also suppress TR during the peak heat generation phase.

F. Water mist's inability to suppress active TR is explained by its peak cooling power being less than 5% of the TR heat generation rate, and nanofluid's peak cooling power, while much larger, still does not approach the TR heat generation rate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states both water mist and nanofluid are difficult to suppress TR during the TR period due to insufficient cooling power relative to heat generation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Nanofluid's faster cooling time and higher cooling rate apply post-TR; evidence does not extend these advantages to active TR suppression.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence links pre-TR suppression to dissipating SEI heat before self-heating, but does not establish nanofluid's 22x natural cooling advantage as sufficient for this; this is an unsupported cross-paper inference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Nanofluid's 6067 W is far below the 2862 W TR heat generation rate claim is reversed; and evidence explicitly states suppression during TR is not achievable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher critical suppression temperature applies to pre-TR or early-stage intervention; evidence does not extend this to active TR peak heat generation phase.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states water mist peak cooling is 4.04% of TR heat generation; nanofluid at 6067 W vs. 2862 W TR rate still does not demonstrate suppression during TR per evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing post-TR cooling performance from active TR suppression capability, and evaluating admissibility of suppression claims based on power balance
analysis.primary_boundary: Cooling performance metrics during post-TR phase cannot be extrapolated to active TR suppression; power balance during TR is the governing constraint
intended_error_modes: regime_transfer_from_post_TR_to_active_TR
numerical_inversion_of_power_comparison
unsupported_pre_TR_suppression_inference
disallowed_extrapolations: Nanofluid's post-TR cooling advantage implies active TR suppression
22x natural cooling ratio implies pre-TR suppression sufficiency

solver_view.prompt: A battery module engineer is designing a forced convective cooling system using air at 1 bar to prevent thermal runaway propagation under 2C discharge. A colleague suggests that switching to argon gas at the same flow speed would provide equivalent or better protection than nitrogen because argon is chemically inert and will not react with battery materials during thermal runaway. Evaluate this suggestion. In your answer, identify the physical property that governs cooling gas effectiveness, explain why argon's inertness does not determine its cooling performance relative to nitrogen, and state what design implication follows from the evidence regarding gas selection.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Cooling gas effectiveness in suppressing thermal runaway propagation is governed primarily by heat capacity, not chemical inertness. Evidence shows nitrogen outperforms argon and air because of its higher heat capacity, while argon exhibits the lowest effectiveness of the three gases due to its lower heat capacity. Argon's chemical inertness is irrelevant to its heat transfer capability; a chemically inert gas with low heat capacity will still transfer less thermal energy per unit mass than a gas with higher heat capacity at identical flow conditions. The design implication is that gas selection should prioritize heat capacity over chemical inertness: nitrogen is the preferred cooling medium among common gases for reducing thermal runaway propagation risk at equivalent flow speeds.
must_include: heat capacity governs cooling effectiveness, not chemical inertness
argon has lower heat capacity than nitrogen and therefore lower cooling effectiveness
nitrogen outperforms both argon and air
chemical inertness of argon does not improve its heat transfer performance
nitrogen is the preferred gas selection for TRP suppression among the compared gases
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming argon's inertness provides any safety benefit for TRP suppression under the stated conditions
Claiming nitrogen completely suppresses TRP under all conditions without specifying flow and pressure requirements
Extending the gas comparison to conditions other than identical flow speed and pressure

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying the physical property governing cooling gas effectiveness and rejecting a plausible but mechanistically incorrect design rationale based on chemical inertness
analysis.primary_boundary: Heat capacity, not chemical reactivity, determines cooling gas performance for TRP suppression; inertness is a separate property with no direct bearing on heat transfer
intended_error_modes: Conflating chemical inertness with thermal safety benefit
Assuming inert gases provide superior protection due to non-reactivity with battery materials
Failing to identify heat capacity as the governing property

solver_view.stem: A parallel-connected 18650 NCM battery pack is subjected to a high-current overcharge event at approximately 3C rate. The pack includes cells both with and without current interrupt devices (CIDs). Engineers observe that CID-equipped cells interrupt current but several still progress to combustion, while the cell lacking a CID reaches thermal runaway significantly faster. Which conclusions about the role of CIDs under this condition are admissible?
options: A. The CID provides a meaningful delay to thermal runaway onset under high-rate overcharge, even though it cannot prevent the eventual escalation of internal side reactions.
B. The CID's current interruption function is sufficient to prevent thermal runaway at high charging rates, provided it activates before the battery voltage reaches the overcharge ceiling.
C. The absence of a CID eliminates the only available current interruption mechanism, causing earlier thermal runaway onset compared to CID-equipped cells under the same high-rate overcharge.
D. At high overcharge rates, the rate of internal heat and gas accumulation can outpace the protective response of the CID, rendering it unable to fully suppress thermal runaway.
E. Because CID-equipped cells still experience combustion at 3C, the CID provides no measurable protective benefit under any overcharge condition.
F. The probability of combustion at 3C overcharge rate is high enough that CID presence alone should not be treated as a sufficient safeguard against thermal runaway at that rate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CID-equipped cells outlasted the no-CID cell, confirming delay even without prevention under high-rate overcharge.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows CID-equipped cells still combusted at 3C; current interruption alone is insufficient to prevent thermal runaway at high rates.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cell_7 without CID exploded at 424 s, significantly earlier than CID-equipped cells, confirming CID absence accelerates onset.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states internal reaction rate exceeds CID response speed under high-rate overcharge, preventing full suppression.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overgeneralizes from high-rate failure; CID successfully prevented thermal runaway under low-current overcharge in all tested cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 80% combustion probability at 3C with CID present supports that CID alone is an insufficient safeguard at that rate.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Regime-dependent effectiveness of CID protection under low vs. high overcharge current rates
analysis.primary_boundary: CID prevents thermal runaway under low-rate overcharge but cannot suppress it under high-rate overcharge due to reaction rate exceeding device response speed
intended_error_modes: protective_mechanism_overclaim
overgeneralization_from_high_rate_failure_to_all_conditions
ignoring_regime_transition
disallowed_extrapolations: CID sufficiency at high rate
CID uselessness at all rates
voltage threshold as sole CID trigger criterion

solver_view.stem: A battery module uses phase change material (PCM) with thermal conductivity of 0.28 W/(m·K) and 4 mm thickness. Engineers consider two independent upgrades: (i) increasing PCM thermal conductivity to 12.0 W/(m·K), and (ii) increasing PCM liquidus temperature from 310 K to 340 K. A single trigger cell undergoes thermal runaway. Which statements about the thermal outcomes of these upgrades are admissible?
options: A. Upgrade (i) can reduce the trigger cell peak temperature sufficiently to inhibit the positive electrode active material reaction with solvent, whereas the baseline conductivity cannot achieve this.
B. Upgrade (ii) will substantially reduce the trigger cell peak temperature because a higher melting point PCM stores more latent heat before transitioning.

C. Upgrade (i) is more effective than upgrade (ii) at reducing peak cell temperature during thermal runaway, because thermal conductivity governs heat removal rate while liquidus temperature has only marginal influence under rapid thermal runaway conditions.
D. Upgrade (ii) prevents thermal runaway propagation to adjacent cells because the higher liquidus temperature increases the total energy absorbed before PCM is fully melted.
E. At 4 mm PCM thickness, further increasing thickness to 6 mm or beyond would provide diminishing returns on peak temperature reduction, suggesting thickness is already near a saturation threshold.
F. Upgrade (i) alone guarantees that no adjacent cell undergoes thermal runaway, because reducing peak temperature below electrolyte decomposition onset eliminates all heat transfer pathways.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At kp=12.0 W/(m·K), Tmax drops to 417 K, below the positive material-solvent reaction threshold; at 0.28 W/(m·K), Tmax is 704 K, above it.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows liquidus temperature increase from 310 to 340 K causes only non-significant changes in peak temperature due to rapid TR dynamics.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Conductivity increase produces large Tmax reductions; liquidus increase produces only slight changes because TR speed allows most PCM to melt regardless.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows liquidus temperature changes produce non-significant effects on propagation metrics; propagation prevention is not supported for upgrade (ii).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that at d≥6 mm, Tmax and T2min no longer change significantly, indicating saturation; 4 mm is within the diminishing-return transition zone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reducing Tmax suppresses reactions but does not eliminate all heat transfer pathways; adjacent cell propagation depends on multiple factors beyond peak temperature alone.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Differential effectiveness of PCM conductivity vs. liquidus temperature on thermal runaway suppression, and PCM thickness saturation behavior
analysis.primary_boundary: Conductivity governs heat removal rate and produces large Tmax changes; liquidus temperature has marginal effect under rapid TR because most PCM melts regardless
intended_error_modes: mechanism_confusion_latent_heat_vs_conductivity
protective_mechanism_overclaim
causal_overclaim_single_variable

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion battery array undergoes thermal runaway in one cell. Without intervention, all remaining cells reach thermal runaway within 5 seconds. Engineers test two water spray strategies: continuous spray (20 s effective duration) and intermittent spray at two different cycling periods—both at the same duty cycle of 0.5. The 5-second period intermittent spray fails to prevent propagation; the 2-second period spray prevents propagation by holding a neighboring cell surface near 100°C. Which conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The failure of the 5-second period spray despite equal duty cycle to the 2-second period spray demonstrates that total water volume delivered is not the sole determinant of propagation suppression.
B. Continuous spray with 20 s duration is the most effective strategy tested because it delivers the highest total heat dissipation over the suppression window.

C. The 2-second period spray succeeds because its average cooling power during the approximately 5-second propagation interval is sufficient to keep the neighboring cell below its critical temperature.

D. Intermittent spray with a 5-second period fails because its off-phase duration matches or exceeds the propagation interval, allowing the neighboring cell to accumulate sufficient heat between spray pulses.
E. Continuous spray prevents thermal runaway propagation by delivering sustained cooling that eliminates the thermal gradient between cells.
F. Suppression effectiveness depends on cooling power delivered during the critical propagation window, not merely on total energy removed over the full intervention duration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Equal duty cycle but different outcomes confirm total volume is insufficient; temporal distribution of cooling power determines outcome.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Continuous spray delayed but did not prevent propagation; 2-second period spray prevented it, making continuous spray not the most effective despite higher total dissipation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 2-second period spray maintained cell #3 near 100°C, well below the ~195°C critical threshold, consistent with sufficient average cooling power during the 5-second window.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A 5-second period spray has an off-phase of 2.5 s at DC=0.5; given the ~5 s propagation interval, the off-phase allows sufficient heat accumulation to drive the cell toward critical temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Continuous spray did not prevent propagation; cell #3 eventually reached critical temperature at 197 s, so thermal gradient elimination is not achieved.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states suppression capability depends on cooling power during the suppression stage, not only total heat dissipation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Role of instantaneous cooling power vs. total heat dissipation in thermal runaway propagation suppression, and interaction with the critical propagation time window
analysis.primary_boundary: Suppression requires sufficient average cooling power during the ~5 s propagation interval; total energy delivered over a longer window is insufficient if instantaneous power is inadequate during that window

solver_view.prompt: A battery module engineer is comparing two design choices for preventing thermal runaway propagation: (A) using a PCM layer with high thermal conductivity between cells, and (B) deploying a water spray system with intermittent activation. Both are intended to protect neighboring cells after one trigger cell undergoes thermal runaway. The engineer assumes that as long as total heat absorbed or removed over the full event duration is maximized, propagation will be prevented. Identify the specific flaw in this assumption, explain the physical mechanism that makes the assumption incorrect, and state what design parameter each strategy must satisfy to reliably prevent propagation.

ground_truth.gold_answer: The assumption is flawed because propagation prevention depends on the rate of heat removal during the critical propagation window, not total energy removed over the full event. For PCM, what matters is whether thermal conductivity is high enough to reduce the neighboring cell peak temperature below reaction-triggering thresholds during the rapid thermal runaway event; a PCM with low conductivity may absorb sufficient total energy but fail to remove heat fast enough to prevent the cell from reaching critical temperature. For water spray, what matters is average cooling power during the short propagation interval (approximately 5 seconds); a spray system delivering the same total water volume over a longer period but with insufficient instantaneous power during that window will fail to prevent propagation even though total heat dissipation is equal or greater. The correct design parameters are: for PCM, thermal conductivity sufficient to suppress peak temperature below critical reaction thresholds; for spray, spray period short enough that average cooling power during the propagation interval keeps the neighboring cell below its critical temperature.
must_include: rate of heat removal during the propagation window, not total energy, governs suppression
PCM thermal conductivity must be sufficient to reduce peak temperature below reaction thresholds
spray cooling power during the critical interval must keep neighboring cell below critical temperature
equal total heat dissipation does not guarantee equal suppression effectiveness
disallowed_extrapolations: claiming PCM thickness alone determines propagation prevention without reference to conductivity
claiming any continuous spray prevents propagation
claiming total water volume or total PCM latent heat is the controlling design parameter
generalizing the 5-second propagation interval to all battery chemistries or configurations

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinction between instantaneous cooling power during the propagation window and total heat dissipation as the governing parameter for propagation suppression in both PCM and spray strategies
analysis.primary_boundary: Total energy absorbed is not equivalent to suppression effectiveness; temporal rate of heat removal during the critical window determines whether neighboring cell temperature is controlled below critical threshold
intended_error_modes: confusing total heat dissipation with suppression effectiveness
ignoring the time-rate constraint imposed by the short propagation interval
treating PCM latent heat capacity as the primary design variable instead of thermal conductivity
treating spray duty cycle or total volume as sufficient design parameters
